text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 12 May 2022 

Dr Helen Ward  
Riverside Health Centre  
48 Wharf Road, London N1 7GR  

Dear Dr Helen Ward,  

Amelia Carter, DOB: 22-09-1991, Hospital No: U847520 NHS No. 0045612398  
Flat 3, 12 Kingfisher Mews, London E2 8QJ 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (patient has both generalised and focal epilepsy)/history of mild head injury in adolescence (no current sequelae)/intermittent musculoskeletal strain related to manual handling at work/smoker (5/day).  

Medication: Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
Clobazam 10 mg nocte PRN during clusters (patient-reported intermittent use).  
Vitamin D 1000 IU daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg od.  

I reviewed Amelia Carter in clinic today. She continues to work as a warehouse picker between tall racking and narrow aisles where lifting tasks and pace vary by shift. She reports that the variability in pace can increase her tiredness by the end of a late shift, though she denies missed doses of medication. Sleep is generally 6–7 hours on early shifts and 7–8 hours on late shifts. No new injuries or absences from work were reported. 

Seizures: The epilepsy phenotype remains combined, with both generalised and focal seizure types. Her last convulsive seizure was recorded in 2/2021, with occasional clusters of myoclonic jerks persisting. She describes these clusters as brief, shock-like jerks of both arms and sometimes the shoulders, typically occurring on awakening after several early starts or following consecutive heavy-lifting shifts; clusters comprise multiple jerks over minutes to an hour and may recur on a few mornings within the same week before settling. In addition, she continues to experience infrequent focal aware sensory auras (tingling in the left hand with a rising epigastric sensation) without evolution to impaired awareness; these occur sporadically and are not associated with convulsions. There have been no episodes of loss of awareness on the warehouse floor, and no recent injuries. She uses clobazam at the onset of a myoclonic cluster with partial benefit and no excessive sedation reported. 

Examination: Alert and oriented, normal speech and language. Cranial nerves intact. Motor exam normal bulk and tone; power full throughout. Coordination intact; no intention tremor. Gait steady including tandem. No focal neurological deficit. 

Investigations: Last MRI (2019) normal. EEG (2020) showed generalized spike–wave discharges with superimposed right frontal sharp activity. Routine bloods (02/2022) normal including LFTs, U&Es, and vitamin D replete. 

Plan:  
- Maintain levetiracetam 1500 mg bd and lamotrigine 150 mg bd unchanged.  
- Continue clobazam 10 mg nocte PRN at the onset of myoclonic clusters; reinforced advice on limited short runs only, not daily.  
- Written advice provided on sleep regularity around changing shifts, hydration, and consistent meal timing to mitigate triggers.  
- Seizure and trigger chart to be maintained, with particular note of shift pattern, sleep hours, and manual-lifting intensity.  
- Occupational safety: avoid climbing to higher racking levels without a spotter during periods of myoclonic clusters; patient to inform supervisor if clusters occur.  
- Follow-up in 9–12 months, sooner if clusters increase in frequency or if any impaired-awareness or convulsive events recur.  

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: Her last convulsive seizure was recorded in 2/2021, with occasional clusters of myoclonic jerks persisting.

attributes.Label: multiple cluster per 15 month, multiple per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: 1) Identify seizure information: The letter states the patient has ongoing "occasional clusters of myoclonic jerks persisting" and "infrequent focal aware sensory auras". It also specifies clusters "comprise multiple jerks over minutes to an hour" and "may recur on a few mornings within the same week before settling." 2) Determine seizure-free status: The patient is not seizure-free overall because clusters and auras persist. 3) Establish a time window tied to the letter: Clinic date is 12 May 2022 and the last convulsive seizure was 2/2021. From Feb 2021 to 12 May 2022 is approximately 1 year and 3 months = 15 months. 4) Quantify frequency using allowed formats: Exact counts per day/week/month/year are not given, but "occasional clusters" across this 15-month period implies more than one cluster occurred in that span (i.e., multiple clusters). The content also explicitly supports that each cluster contains multiple jerks. 5) Apply the highest-frequency rule: Among described seizure types, clusters have the clearest pattern and are ongoing; auras are "infrequent" without quantifiable rate. Therefore, represent the cluster frequency over the 15-month interval with intracluster multiplicity.
seizure_frequency_number: multiple cluster per 15 month, multiple per cluster
